source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Air in an automobile tire is initially at $-10 \, ^{\circ} ext{C}$ and $190 \, \text{kPa}$. After the automobile is driven awhile, the temperature rises to $10 \, ^{\circ} ext{C}$. You must make one assumption on your own. Find the new pressure.

image_dependency: Question text appears self-contained and gives all numerical values needed for a gas-law calculation.
Input metadata says requires_image=true and text_dominant=false, which suggests some visual association, but no explicit reference to a diagram, graph, or spatial feature appears in the text.
Registry shows one image exists and registry_passed=true, but image necessity is not evidenced from the question wording itself.
multi_step: Solving requires making an unstated assumption ('one assumption on your own'), likely constant volume, before applying a relation.
The temperatures are given in Celsius, so a correct solution typically requires conversion to absolute temperature and then computing the new pressure.
This is more than direct lookup, but still a short physics calculation with limited branching.
verifiability: Answer is a specific numeric short_text value ('204.4 kPa'), which is easy to check.
Multiple-choice options are provided, enabling exact final-answer verification.
Given the stated quantities and a standard assumption, the computation path is structured and auditable; registry_passed=true and answer_present=true.

decision_reasons: Question, answer, and primary image are all present, and the asset registry passed with no recorded issues.
The sample is highly verifiable: it has a specific numeric short-text answer and supporting multiple-choice structure.
Scoring evidence indicates the question text is largely self-contained, so core semantics appear recoverable even if image necessity is ambiguous.
Risk flags exist (implicit assumption required and metadata conflict about image dependence), but they do not undermine the availability of the core problem content.
